title: 2  Prelude

title: 2  Prelude
section: 2.1 Why Emotional Intelligence in Data Science—and Why Now
text:
2.1 Why Emotional Intelligence in Data Science—and Why Now
After years of academic work in statistics, measurement, and cognitive science—particularly in learning, development, assessment, and standardized testing—I transitioned into statistical consulting. This shift was not merely a change in professional setting; it was a change in what effective practice required.
My training in statistical theory and methodological rigor provided a strong foundation for consulting across diverse domains, including medicine, education, entrepreneurship, public health, and industry. Yet, as my consulting work expanded, a consistent pattern became increasingly clear: technical expertise alone was not sufficient. A data scientist or statistical consultant may be highly skilled in modeling, inference, and computation, but still fail to produce real-world impact—especially when navigating real clients, real constraints, and high-stakes decisions.
Over time, I came to understand that effective applied work requires a broader set of competencies. Beyond theoretical knowledge and programming proficiency, success depends on interpersonal and interpretive capacities: listening carefully, communicating clearly, managing conflict, and explaining statistical findings in ways that are meaningful to non-technical stakeholders. Equally essential is the ability to recognize ethical dilemmas, resist inappropriate pressures, and uphold integrity when results are inconvenient, misunderstood, or contested.
This book grew out of that realization. It is grounded in lived experience—both successes and difficult lessons—that made the role of emotional intelligence in data science concrete and unavoidable.

title: 2  Prelude
section: 2.2 Lessons That Made EQ Tangible in Consulting
text:
2.2 Lessons That Made EQ Tangible in Consulting

2.2.1 Active Listening: A Most Underrated Tool
Early in my consulting work, I often approached client meetings with a familiar academic mindset: define the problem quickly, identify an appropriate method, and move toward a solution. I soon learned that this impulse—however well-intentioned—was frequently counterproductive.
In consulting, the central task is not simply selecting a technique; it is correctly understanding the problem. Consultants often work outside their subject-matter expertise. When collaborating with surgeons, entrepreneurs, educators, or industry professionals, I had to devote substantial time to learning domain language and practical constraints shaping the research question. I learned that a consultant cannot assume shared terminology, shared assumptions, or shared definitions of success.
One practice became essential: after listening carefully and clarifying key terms and relationships, I would restate the problem in writing and return it to the client for confirmation before any analysis began. This habit—simple in appearance—often determined whether a project proceeded productively or became misdirected.






NoteLesson Learned



A successful data scientist is not only a technical solver but also a translator of human questions into statistical questions—and that translation begins with active listening.




2.2.2 Context Matters: Communication Is Not an Optional Add-On
A second lesson emerged while evaluating a life-skills program in a large high school serving approximately 4,000 students. One program objective was to teach ninth graders “conflict resolution.” The curriculum consisted of a pamphlet outlining steps and strategies for managing conflict, which instructors were expected to teach.
Classroom observations revealed that identical content produced markedly different outcomes depending on how it was delivered. In one classroom, the teacher reviewed the steps in a procedural manner while students remained disengaged or disruptive. In another classroom, a teacher introduced conflict resolution through historical contexts and reflective journaling, asking students to connect the steps to conflicts in their own lives. The contrast was striking: when instruction was embedded in meaning and context, students engaged; when presented abstractly, the concept remained detached and ineffective.
This reinforced a principle that applies directly to data science: findings and recommendations must be communicated in context to be understood and used. An analysis that is technically correct but not interpretable or actionable for stakeholders is functionally incomplete.






TipLesson Learned



Data science is not only computation; it is meaning-making. Statistical conclusions must be communicated in accessible language and grounded in the realities of the decision environment.




2.2.3 Teamwork Requires Skill, Not Assumption
As teamwork became increasingly emphasized across educational levels—shaped in part by research on cooperative learning—I observed a recurring misconception: many students interpret “teamwork” as dividing tasks, working independently, and assembling results at the end. This approach often produces fragmentation rather than collaboration.
Research on cooperative learning highlights the importance of shared understanding, mutual accountability, and structured coordination. Effective teams require communication norms, conflict-resolution strategies, and collaborative leadership—not simply division of labor.






TipLesson Learned



Collaboration is not automatic. Team success requires interpersonal competence, particularly under ambiguity and pressure, which are characteristic of real-world analytics.




2.2.4 Locus of Control and Construct Validity: When Context Redefines Measurement
Another formative example occurred while evaluating a program in communities affected by substantial gang activity. The program aimed to strengthen students’ internal locus of control and to develop conflict-resolution skills. I was asked to develop a survey to measure these constructs.
After reviewing the literature (in an era prior to tools such as ChatGPT), I drafted items aligned with theory and prepared a scoring key. Before administration, I shared the instrument with teachers who understood the students’ lived context. One item read:
“If I get into a conflict with somebody, I pretend like nothing happened.”
In classic conflict-resolution frameworks, endorsement might be interpreted as avoidance and scored as undesirable. However, teachers immediately challenged this interpretation. In their setting, students were often advised to avoid confrontation for safety—to walk away and “pretend nothing happened.” The theoretically “correct” scoring did not map cleanly onto practical reality.
This moment underscored a central principle: measurement is not merely technical; it is contextual. Without genuine dialogue with practitioners—and humility about one’s own content knowledge—an evaluator can produce an instrument that is theoretically coherent yet practically invalid.






TipLesson Learned



Strong practice requires continuous communication with clients and serious attention to context, culture, and meaning. Valid measurement depends on shared interpretation.

title: 2  Prelude
section: 2.3 The Industry Message That Intensified the Need
text:
2.3 The Industry Message That Intensified the Need
Over the years, I repeatedly heard a striking comment from industry professionals:
“Universities don’t train students to operate in the real world. We have to send them to workshops to learn communication, collaboration, and leadership.”
This concern is not universally true, but it reflects a persistent gap that emerges when technical training is treated as complete preparation. Students deserve more than learning essential workplace competencies through trial-and-error, anxiety, and avoidable setbacks.
This book is therefore also a response to professional responsibility. We train students to participate in consequential decisions across medicine, law, education, finance, and public policy. Technical proficiency is necessary—but not sufficient—for confident, ethical, and effective practice.

title: 2  Prelude
section: 2.4 Ethical Practice: The Missing Course in Many Data Science Pathways
text:
2.4 Ethical Practice: The Missing Course in Many Data Science Pathways
Consulting made the centrality of ethics impossible to ignore. In my own graduate training in statistics, formal education in professional ethics was limited—despite the fact that fields such as medicine and law treat ethics as foundational.
In contemporary practice, data-based decisions shape high-stakes outcomes. Ethical reasoning is not an optional add-on; it is part of competent professional practice. Consulting also exposes analysts to pressures—sometimes subtle, sometimes explicit—to distort interpretations, privilege statistical significance over substantive meaning, or frame results to support predetermined agendas.
One experience from approximately twenty-five years ago remains vivid. I was evaluating a large-scale program with thousands of student participants. Program leadership was focused on achieving statistical significance because funding decisions depended on it. With a large sample, nearly all results reached statistical significance. However, when it became clear that observed changes were not practically meaningful, the response shifted from celebration to frustration.
When I raised concerns about ceiling effects, social desirability bias, and measurement limitations, the conversation became difficult. In effect, the desire for “significant results” had become stronger than the desire to understand reality. I ultimately stepped away from the project. That experience reinforced the importance of preparing students not only to “do the right thing,” but also to anticipate ethical pressure and navigate the professional consequences of maintaining integrity.






TipLesson Learned



Ethical competence is part of technical competence. A well-rounded data scientist must be prepared to recognize pressure, defend integrity, and communicate truth responsibly.

title: 2  Prelude
section: 2.5 From Consulting Practice to a Training Model
text:
2.5 From Consulting Practice to a Training Model
These experiences—combined with my background in cognitive science, learning, and measurement—led me to propose a model for preparing data scientists for real-world success. The model emphasizes five domains:

Theoretical Knowledge of Statistics
Programming Skills
Communication and Team-Building

active listening
empathy
conflict resolution
shared accountability in collaboration

Statistical Literacy

explaining statistical findings in context for non-technical audiences

Ethical Practice of Data Science

This framework has shaped my teaching as well. More than a decade ago, we began offering a four-unit undergraduate course at UCLA titled The Practice of Statistical Consulting. Over time, enrollment grew substantially (recently exceeding 300 students in a single academic year), and the course was revised repeatedly to reflect what students need when they move from classroom exercises to authentic client-based work. Eventually, the course evolved into a two-course sequence: one emphasizing foundational interpersonal and professional competencies, and a second emphasizing the theoretical and practical demands of real consulting projects.
This book extends that work. Its purpose is to make explicit what is often left implicit: the strongest data scientists integrate intellectual rigor with interpersonal intelligence, contextual understanding, and ethical judgment.

title: 2  Prelude
section: 2.6 The Missing Link: Connecting Mathematical and Applied Statistics
text:
2.6 The Missing Link: Connecting Mathematical and Applied Statistics
One of the most persistent challenges I have observed in teaching is that many students do not perceive the conceptual continuity between mathematical statistics and applied statistics. A familiar example is analysis of variance (ANOVA). Students often experience ANOVA as a collection of formulas rather than as a coherent framework for modeling variation. As a result, they may not recognize that ANOVA and multiple linear regression are instances of the same general linear model, differing primarily in how predictors are encoded—ANOVA emphasizing categorical predictors (and their interactions), and regression accommodating continuous predictors as well.
This disconnect becomes especially apparent when students are asked to interpret variance decomposition. For example, when asked to quantify the proportion of variance attributable to a factor, many do not immediately recognize the conceptual simplicity of the ratio \(`SS_{\text{factor}}/SS_{\text{total}}`\). The mathematical objects are present in their coursework, but the interpretive bridge to meaning, inference, and decision-making is often underdeveloped.
From the perspective of cognitive science, this gap is unsurprising. Knowledge becomes durable and transferable when it is meaningfully connected to prior knowledge. Without explicit opportunities for synthesis—linking established theoretical structures to new applications—students may learn procedures in isolation and struggle to generalize their understanding to unfamiliar contexts.
In my experience, the consulting classroom is one of the most effective environments for cultivating this synthesis because it requires students to solve authentic problems for real stakeholders. In that setting, concepts that were previously compartmentalized across courses must be integrated into a coherent analytic strategy.
Consider a common modeling scenario: a student includes a categorical predictor with sixteen levels (e.g., ethnic background) in a regression model predicting perceived academic success. The model produces fifteen coefficients, several with large standard errors due to small subgroup sample sizes. Yet students may not begin with a simple frequency table or ask whether the data support such a complex parameterization. Despite prior instruction in exploratory analysis and modeling, they do not always apply those habits under real constraints such as sparse data.
More broadly, many students have limited experience reasoning through questions such as:

What happens to the stability and interpretability of estimates when sample size is small relative to model complexity?
How should modeling strategies change under rare events or severe class imbalance?
Should an interaction term remain if it explains only a marginal proportion of variance?
What constitutes a “good” \(`R^{2}`\)in the social sciences, and why does it differ across domains?
Why is \(`R^{2}`\)not an appropriate measure of accuracy in classification problems?

Without authentic practice, students can default to pattern-matching—replicating code, following cookbook rules, and producing output without developing an integrated analytic approach. Consulting disrupts this tendency by requiring students to unify theoretical knowledge, applied reasoning, communication, collaboration, and ethical judgment.
Finally, I have observed an important assessment-related pattern. Team projects often receive high scores, and appropriately so: effective teamwork can yield strong deliverables. However, one-on-one meetings sometimes reveal substantial variation in statistical understanding across team members. With the growing availability of automated code generation and online tools, students can assemble analyses that appear sophisticated without fully understanding the underlying theory.
This observation has shaped my own teaching practices. After years of assigning take-home exams, I returned to closed-book examinations with a single student-prepared reference sheet. This approach better distinguishes group performance from individual mastery, makes conceptual gaps visible, and helps students identify specific areas requiring deeper development.






TipLesson Learned



Durable statistical understanding requires explicit, repeated opportunities to synthesize theory with application in authentic, constraint-driven contexts.








TipLesson Learned



Assessment should separate polished collaborative outputs from individual conceptual mastery to reveal—and remediate—hidden gaps in understanding.

title: 2  Prelude
text:
2.7 Conclusion
Taken together, these observations point to a central premise of this book: technical competence is necessary, but often insufficient for effective practice. What students frequently lack is not exposure to additional methods, but sustained opportunities to integrate statistical theory with applied reasoning, professional judgment, and clear communication under real-world constraints. The consulting classroom makes that integration unavoidable. It requires students to articulate assumptions, evaluate tradeoffs, confront uncertainty, and translate results into decisions that matter to stakeholders who may not share the language of statistical formalism.
For this reason, I treat emotional intelligence not as an accessory to data science, but as part of its professional infrastructure. The ability to listen carefully, ask precise questions, navigate disagreement, communicate limitations, and act ethically strengthens analytic rigor and increases the likelihood that statistical work leads to sound decisions. The chapters that follow are organized around this integrative view—developing the competencies that connect mathematical foundations to applied impact, and preparing readers to practice data science in a manner that is technically sound, contextually grounded, and broadly intelligible.

title: 2  Prelude
text:
3.2 Who This Book Is For
This book is written for readers who already understand (or are learning) the technical foundations of data science and want to become effective, trusted professionals in real-world settings.

3.2.1 Primary audiences

Undergraduate and graduate students in statistics, data science, biostatistics, and related fields
Early-career data scientists, analysts, and statisticians transitioning from coursework to stakeholder-driven work
Experienced practitioners who want to strengthen communication, collaboration, and ethical judgment in high-stakes environments
Instructors designing consulting courses, practicum experiences, capstone courses, or professional-skills modules



3.2.2 Secondary audiences

Project leads, managers, and principal investigators supervising analytics teams
Domain experts (e.g., clinicians, educators, policy staff, product leaders) who collaborate with data scientists and want a shared language for good practice

title: 2  Prelude
section: 3.3 How This Book Can Be Used
text:
3.3 How This Book Can Be Used

3.3.1 For students and self-study

Read each chapter with a “practice lens”: extract one skill, apply it in a real or simulated project, and reflect on what changed.
Build a portfolio: keep your memos, decision briefs, and ethical pre-mortems as evidence of professional readiness.



3.3.2 For courses and workshops

Use the end-of-part capstones as graded deliverables. Each capstone can be completed independently or combined into a single integrated portfolio.
Run short in-class role-plays using the scripts and meeting-note templates to practice conflict resolution and pushback.



3.3.3 For professional teams

Adopt the templates as standard operating practice: problem restatement memo, decision log, decision brief, and documentation checklist.
Use the ethical tools proactively (ethics pre-mortem) and defensively (integrity memo) to protect validity and prevent harm.

Optional implementation: instructors can require a “deliverable bundle” per project milestone (kickoff, midstream findings, final recommendation).

title: 2  Prelude
section: 3.4 Conclusion: Technical Rigor Requires Human Rigor
text:
3.4 Conclusion: Technical Rigor Requires Human Rigor
The central claim of this prelude is straightforward: technical competence is necessary, but often insufficient for effective practice. In real settings, data scientists must translate human questions into statistical questions, communicate results in context, collaborate under ambiguity, and uphold integrity when incentives reward convenience over truth. These capabilities are not accessories to data science; they are part of its professional infrastructure. The chapters that follow therefore treat emotional intelligence, statistical literacy, and ethical judgment as competencies that strengthen analytic rigor—not as “soft” add-ons. A well-rounded data scientist is defined by what their work enables: sound decisions, clear understanding of uncertainty, and responsible action under real constraints.

title: 2  Prelude
section: Shared case scenario (used across all capstones)
text:
Shared case scenario (used across all capstones)
A school district is piloting a new STEM-focused mathematics program for eighth graders across multiple schools. Students are taught in intact classrooms (random assignment is not feasible). Leadership wants evidence to decide whether to scale the program district-wide next year. You are the data scientist/statistical consultant supporting the evaluation. You have a tight timeline, multiple stakeholder groups, and nontrivial measurement and fairness concerns.

title: 2  Prelude
text:
Capstone 1: Theoretical Knowledge of Statistics

Learning objectives

Select an appropriate evaluation design given nonrandom assignment and articulate its assumptions.
Define estimands (what effect is being estimated) and justify unit of analysis and inference strategy.
Identify major threats to validity and propose robustness checks.



Estimated time
90–120 minutes (individual) or 60–90 minutes (team)


Task

Write a 1–2 page Analysis Plan: problem statement, estimand(s), proposed model/design, and key assumptions.
Include at least two validity threats and at least two robustness checks (e.g., sensitivity analysis, subgroup stability, alternative specifications).



Deliverables

Analysis Plan (1–2 pages)
Assumptions and Definitions Log (living table, one page minimum)



Rubric










Criteria
Exemplary (3)
Proficient (2)
Developing (1)
Needs work (0)




Design choice and justification
Design fits constraints; assumptions are explicit and aligned to the decision context.
Design is reasonable; assumptions are mostly stated but may lack precision.
Design is partially mismatched or weakly justified; assumptions are vague.
Design is inappropriate or unjustified; assumptions missing.


Validity threats and robustness plan
Identifies key threats; proposes concrete, feasible checks that address them.
Identifies major threats; checks are plausible but incomplete.
Mentions threats; checks are generic or weakly connected.
Threats/checks largely absent or irrelevant.


Clarity of estimand and interpretation
Clearly defines what effect is estimated and what it means in practice.
Defines effect with minor ambiguities; interpretation mostly clear.
Effect definition is unclear; interpretation is confusing.
No clear estimand; interpretation not defensible.


Professional structure and readability
Well organized; decision-ready language; concise and coherent.
Generally organized; minor clarity issues.
Hard to follow; excessive jargon or missing structure.
Disorganized; not usable by stakeholders.

title: 2  Prelude
text:
Capstone 2: Programming Skills

Learning objectives

Build a reproducible analysis pipeline with clear data checks and versioning.
Implement diagnostics and produce outputs that can be regenerated end-to-end.
Document decisions in code and README so a peer can rerun the work.



Estimated time
90–150 minutes


Task

Create a minimal, reproducible project structure (folders, scripts/notebooks).
Implement data validation (missingness, ranges, duplicates), a baseline model, and at least one robustness check.
Write a README with exact steps to reproduce results.



Deliverables

Repository or folder with code/notebook + outputs
README (repro steps + dependencies)
Diagnostics checklist (completed)



Rubric










Criteria
Exemplary (3)
Proficient (2)
Developing (1)
Needs work (0)




Reproducibility
Peer can rerun from scratch with no gaps; versions and dependencies are explicit.
Mostly reproducible; minor missing steps or dependencies.
Partially reproducible; key steps unclear.
Not reproducible; missing files/steps.


Data checks and QA
Comprehensive sanity checks; issues are logged and addressed.
Checks present; some issues not handled or documented.
Minimal checks; likely to miss major issues.
Checks absent.


Code quality and organization
Clear structure; readable functions; comments explain decisions.
Generally readable; some clutter or inconsistent structure.
Difficult to read or navigate; limited documentation.
Unstructured; hard to interpret or reuse.


Diagnostics and robustness
Includes baseline + diagnostics + at least one meaningful robustness check.
Includes baseline and some diagnostics; robustness is limited.
Diagnostics superficial; robustness unclear.
No diagnostics/robustness.

title: 2  Prelude
text:
Capstone 3: Communication and Team-Building

Learning objectives

Demonstrate active listening through accurate paraphrase and confirmation.
Create shared accountability via roles, norms, and conflict protocol.
Document decisions and follow-ups to prevent misalignment.



Estimated time
60–90 minutes (team recommended)


Task

Draft a Problem Restatement Memo to confirm scope, definitions, and success criteria.
Create a Team Charter and a one-page Meeting Notes log using listening loops.
Populate a Decision Log with at least five decisions made during the exercise.



Deliverables

Problem Restatement Memo
Team Charter + Meeting Notes (listening loops)
Decision Log (minimum five entries)



Rubric










Criteria
Exemplary (3)
Proficient (2)
Developing (1)
Needs work (0)




Active listening and problem translation
Paraphrases are precise; definitions clarified; alignment achieved before analysis.
Paraphrases mostly accurate; some definitions remain vague.
Limited confirmation; translation is incomplete or ambiguous.
No evidence of listening loop or alignment.


Team norms and accountability
Clear roles, cadence, review process, and conflict protocol; shared accountability is operationalized.
Most norms present; accountability plan is reasonable.
Norms incomplete; accountability is assumed rather than designed.
No clear norms or accountability.


Documentation quality
Decisions, owners, and dates are clearly recorded; follow-ups are actionable.
Documentation present with minor gaps.
Documentation inconsistent; owners/dates often missing.
Little to no documentation.


Professional tone and usability
Artifacts are stakeholder-ready and easy to use.
Mostly usable; minor clarity issues.
Hard to use; excessive jargon or missing structure.
Not usable in practice.

title: 2  Prelude
section: Capstone 4: Statistical Literacy (Meaning-Making for Non-Technical Audiences)
text:
Capstone 4: Statistical Literacy (Meaning-Making for Non-Technical Audiences)

Learning objectives

Translate statistical results into decision-relevant meaning and uncertainty.
Distinguish statistical significance from practical significance.
Communicate limitations and boundary conditions clearly.



Estimated time
60–120 minutes


Task

Create a 1-page Decision Brief (or 5-slide equivalent) aimed at non-technical leadership.
Include an Impact Table translating effects into practical terms (and cost of being wrong).
Write boundary statements: what you can and cannot conclude.



Deliverables

Decision Brief (1 page or 5 slides)
Impact Table



Rubric










Criteria
Exemplary (3)
Proficient (2)
Developing (1)
Needs work (0)




Interpretation and practical meaning
Findings translated into clear practical implications; uncertainty is integrated.
Interpretation mostly clear; some practical implications are underdeveloped.
Interpretation is overly technical or incomplete.
Interpretation is misleading or absent.


Handling of uncertainty and limitations
Uncertainty and limitations are explicit, honest, and decision-relevant.
Limitations stated; uncertainty partially integrated.
Limitations are generic; uncertainty minimized or unclear.
Limitations/uncertainty omitted.


Audience fit and clarity
Language is accessible; structure supports decision-making.
Generally accessible; minor jargon persists.
Too technical or poorly structured for the audience.
Not audience-appropriate.


Decision readiness
Clear recommendation and next steps with tradeoffs.
Recommendation present; tradeoffs partially addressed.
Recommendation vague; next steps unclear.
No actionable recommendation.

title: 2  Prelude
section: Capstone 5: Ethical Practice of Data Science
text:
Capstone 5: Ethical Practice of Data Science

Learning objectives

Anticipate misuse and harm through an ethics pre-mortem.
Respond to pressure ethically and professionally using defensible language.
Document concerns and define escalation pathways.



Estimated time
60–120 minutes


Task

Complete an Ethics Pre-Mortem: identify at least five ways results could be misused or misinterpreted and propose mitigations.
Write an Integrity Memo responding to a scenario where leadership asks you to ‘emphasize significance’ despite weak practical impact.
Draft a Pushback Script and a Documentation & Escalation Checklist for the same scenario.



Deliverables

Ethics Pre-Mortem
Integrity Memo
Pushback Script + Documentation & Escalation Checklist



Rubric










Criteria
Exemplary (3)
Proficient (2)
Developing (1)
Needs work (0)




Risk identification and mitigation
Identifies realistic risks; mitigations are specific and implementable.
Identifies major risks; mitigations are plausible but not fully developed.
Risks are generic; mitigations are vague.
Risks/mitigations absent or superficial.


Integrity under pressure
Response is firm, evidence-based, and professionally framed; protects validity and stakeholders.
Response is principled; may lack clarity or firmness in places.
Response is hesitant or overly adversarial; defensibility unclear.
Response compromises integrity or is unprofessional.


Documentation and escalation readiness
Clear documentation plan; escalation pathway is appropriate and actionable.
Documentation plan present; escalation somewhat unclear.
Documentation incomplete; escalation plan minimal.
No documentation/escalation plan.


Ethical communication
Boundary statements are clear; avoids misleading claims; communicates uncertainty responsibly.
Mostly responsible; minor overclaiming risk.
Mixed messages; boundary statements weak.
Overclaims or invites misuse.

title: 3  Active Listening
text:
3.1 Mindful Listening
A generation ago a book titled “All I Really Need to Know I Learned in Kinder- garten” topped the bestseller list[@fulghum1986all]. For most of us, listening to others was taught when we entered our first classroom. If we were too chatty or restless we were told to quiet ourselves. We were told to “pay attention.” In schools, listening is generally “taught” by “osmosis” even though it accounts for a plurality (just under 50%) of our language skills[@oxford1993research]. In addition, this vital skill is relatively poorly understood as compared with speaking, reading, and writing. There are many reasons for this neglect[@osada2004listening] we might distill them into three:
ingly uniform. * The spoken word and the written word differ greatly in formality thus listening requires greater mental agility
We approach listening in the context of team building in a statistical consulting en- vironment through a mindfulness approach. With an emphasis on paying full attention to teammates and clients as opposed to hurrying or multitasking.
We will begin with a discussion of building the scaffolding for mindful listening.

title: 3  Active Listening
text:
3.1.1 Mindfulness is awareness.
As an example, when dieting, one strategy is “mindful eating” where the dieter is instructed to look beyond eating as simply inserting food in the one’s mouth and instead to be aware of other factors associated with eating. Thirst is such a factor. Thirst can be mistaken for hunger and without thinking, a person might eat. Mindful eating would inform the dieter “to drink a cup or two of water” and then “wait” before making the decision to eat.


3.1.2 Mindful listening then involves more than hearing with the ears.
From the textbooks of kindergarten teachers and speech language pathologists[@truesdale1990whole]. We will identify its components as:

Look at the person who is speaking to you

Listen with both of your ears

Turn your body towards the other person

Keep your lips and extremities still while the other person is speaking

Thinking about what they are saying

Care about what they are saying

And, for adults: * Keep your devices out of reach
When we speak, we want our listeners to be tuned-in, we want them to connect with us, to correctly understand our problem, or learn about our proposed solutions or insights. It is the rare individual who accomplishes this without actively listening.
Consider for a moment what happens when there is no listening between client and consulting team or within a consulting team. Even the most fundamental of tasks – determining who exactly is on the team – is doomed if there is no listening.

title: 3  Active Listening
text:
3.2 Training Goals Related to Listening
Perhaps the discipline most attuned to the need for listening training is medicine.
Doctors are diagnosticians and healers, AND they possess critical information needed by patients to make vital decisions. While communications training can be found in many medical school programs, it is frequently an elective and sometimes offered only occasionally. Exemplary programs exist and we have adapted some of their strategies, specifically the model presented by the Massachusetts Medical Schools’ Collaborative[@reidy2022paving]
Five competencies are presented that a medical student should possess, we adapt this structure for our consultants:

Consultants should start by assessing the client’s understanding of their own problem and determining their priorities in order to develop an analysis plan.
Consultants should develop their skill of responding in such a way as to develop connection with the client
Consultants should observe and practice conversations related to restating the client’s problem and propose solutions. First in a low stakes learning environment (Stats 140XP) then later on, supervised (Stats 141XP) with moderated real-world clients
Consultants should be equipped with skills assessments and reflective practice essential for developing effective communication
Consultants should be able to field questions and explain the principles and prac- tice of statistics to clients when presenting deliverables The competencies will be addressed as we turn to building listening skills.

title: 3  Active Listening
text:
3.3 Building Listening Skills
Listening is multidimensional and involves both our eyes and ears. Consider the fol- lowing aspects of a speaker’s delivery from a listener’s perspective:

speed
social context (e.g., asking someone to repeat in a private conversation vs a large auditorium)
general vocabulary
non-verbal cues
context/content of the discussion
potential lack of a common language (e.g., non-native speakers but also math or specialized vocabulary)
listeners deficits (e.g., lack of sleep, illness, emotions)


3.3.1 Minimizing Distractions, Maximizing Attention
A core support of active listening is attending. In a consulting environment, the con- sultant cannot be become bored or find the client dull as boredom has the power to activate the pain receptors in our brains[@nederkoorn2016self]. In response, we become distracted and lose focus. Consider the list above – anything that might distract us, for example: missing a word from a rapid-fire speaker, not having a good grasp of the content – will draw us away from understanding a client’s needs.
We training our consulting teams to prepare for meetings by encouraging

turning off electronic devices
making the conscious choice to find the speaker interesting
focusing on the words of the client, not the client’s personal appearance
take occasional notes or if the speaker allows it, record the meeting for review
use one’s thinking speed, which is far more rapid than human speech[@korba1990rate] to anticipate the client’s points or to evaluate what has been presented.



3.3.2 The Vocabulary of the Client


3.3.3 Eudaimonia - or what we can control
Epictetus the stoic taught that the road to happiness could be found in these two things:

Identifying (correctly) what is within our control, and what is not.

Investing our energy on the things within our control and learning to cede that which is not.

We can prepare our consultants for our end of the problem (e.g., getting enough sleep before an important meeting) but recognizing that we cannot control every situa- tion. Acknowledgement of the unexpected goes a long way towards successful consulting by helping the consultant maintain composure in every situation.

title: 3  Active Listening
text:
3.4 Overlap with other constructs
Statistical consulting teams share group fundamentals with other disciplines. * A need for well defined roles and responsibilities * A purpose and an end * A well defined structure (e.g., tasks, agreed upon code of conduct) * Guidance from managers, directors, and clients Communication and empathy are strongly tied with listening. Listening is just as vital (if not more) than speaking. Active listening helps us to understand what is being said and can help prevent miscommunication.

title: 3  Active Listening
text:
3.5 The cross-cultural/social issues.
Research on the premature departure of international workers from corporate environ- ments is revealing[@jassawalla2004cross]. There is difficulty in establishing trust or developing relationships. Inability to clearly communicate difficulties or to seek assistance in a foreign environ- ment can lead to early departures.

title: 3  Active Listening
text:
3.6 EXERCISES

3.6.1 Individual exercises

3.6.1.1 Emotion Review.
This can be practiced when alone and in a calm state of mind.
In a quiet place, think about a recent meaningful (not necessarily work related) conversation. Recall any events that caused an emotional reaction from you.
Now try to think of ways in which you could have handled the situation differently. Example:
Incident: Your friend responds to you in anger because you forgot about a lunch date.
Emotion triggered: Fear. You felt ashamed and had nothing to say so you chose not to respond.
Possible alternative response: Remorse. Express remorse with a sincere apology.
Lessons: To create a sense of empathy, required for mindful listening and to develop emotional intelligence by learning to generate additional possibilities in any situation.


3.6.1.2 Explore the “SOLER” system
The acronym “SOLER” can help you develop your active listening skills.

Sit squarely. Move your body so you face the speaker.
Open posture. Whether you’re sitting or standing, keep your hands open and your arms at your sides. Avoid tightening your body or folding your arms and legs when listening. Those body language signals defensiveness, frustration or impatience.
Lean toward the speaker. This posture demonstrates you are interested in what the speaker is saying.
Eye contact. Maintain comfortable eye contact. A good rule of thumb is to maintain eye contact for about five to 10 seconds, gaze away and look back again.
Relax . The more relaxed you seem the more comfortable the speaker feels.




3.6.2 Group Exercises.

3.6.2.1 Drawing Questions
Have the participants pair up and seat themselves back to back. Hand one member of each pair a drawing (simple) do not show it to the other member. The participant without the drawing is given a blank sheet and something to draw with (pencil, pen, etc.). The “artist” needs to ask questions about the drawing and then draw what they are hearing from their partner. At the end of the activity both are compared to see how accurately the drawing has been replicated.


3.6.2.2 Lessons

The value of clarifying questions and also of framing and asking the right questions.



3.6.2.3 Ask ‘n’ Tell
Divide your participants into groups of size six or more. Ask a volunteer to share a short story related to a major turning point in their life] So for example, when they had to call “911”. Divide the remainder of the group into “askers” and “tellers.” The Askers” are to ONLY ask questions of the volunteer speaker and the “Tellers” are to tell the group about their comparable experience. Ask the group to find another volunteer and ask the listeners to switch their roles (askers should try telling, tellers should try asking).


3.6.2.4 Lessons

Ask the volunteers when they felt heard – when the “Askers” asked or when the “Tellers” told? Ask the group members to discuss some of their feelings around the responses.




3.6.3 For reflection and discussion - individual and/or group

Recall your interactions with people who are/were “good listeners”, what did you find most engaging or memorable about their conversation(s) with you?
Suppose you have been introduced to a new co-worker consider/reflect upon some aspects mindful listening that might be a challenge for the typical person on first meeting.
Can you recall an example, e.g., fictitious, a personal experience or even a historical one, where better listening was important. For example, a mid-air accident was caused by an air controller who thought he heard the word “passed” instead of the word “passing” and misdirected a plane with fatal consequences.
What suggestions can you offer to someone who wants to improve active listening abilities?
Is there a relationship between active listening and conflict resolution? If yes, how do listening skills relate to conflict resolution? If no, can you provide an example?

title: 4  Final Chapter. Ethics in Statistics and Data Science
text:
5 PRINCIPLE A: Professional Integrity and Accountability
1.1 Elaboration and example of ASA Ethical Guidelines for Individuals and Organizations Engaged in the Practice of Statistics Prepared by the Committee on Professional Ethics of the American Statistical Association Approved by ASA Board of Directors February 1, 2022
Purpose of the Guidelines
The American Statistical Association’s Ethical Guidelines for Statistical Practice are intended to help statistical practitioners make decisions ethically. In these guidelines, “statistical practice” includes activities such as …
Professional integrity and accountability require taking responsibility for one’s work. Ethical statistical practice supports valid and prudent decision-making with appropriate methodology. The ethical statistical practitioner represents their capabilities and activities honestly and treats others with respect.
The ethical statistical practitioner:
1.Takes responsibility for evaluating potential tasks, assessing whether they have (or can attain) sufficient competence to execute each task and that the work and timeline are feasible. Does not solicit or deliver work for which they are not qualified or that they would not be willing to have peer reviewed.
2. Uses methodology and data that are valid, relevant, and appropriate, without favoritism or prejudice, and in a manner intended to produce valid, interpretable, and reproducible results.
Example of interpretable results
One of the members on the board wanted to explain the results of the breast cancer study on mass media so that it could be followed by non-statisticians. Logistic regression was used to examine the likelihood of doing a mammography as a function of multiple factors such as knowledge of breast cancer, cultural background, level of education etc. Simple odds ratios within context with no mention of the technicalities were used to explain the findings to the board member.
Example of lack of prejudice
In order to learn more about the beliefs of the Muslim women residing in southern California toward pre-screening for breast cancer, one-on-one interviews were done. The results were so different depending on the country of origin and spoken language. Some women said I must have done something bad and now I am being punished. Others said I do not want anybody to know because my friends will leave me. Others would say, I want to let everyone know and this is something random that happened to me. These one-on-one interviews helped us in understanding and appreciating the differences in attitudes of women from different backgrounds toward pre-screening for breast cancer and how they felt after they had the cancer . These experiences are very helpful in developing lack of prejudice and helping in future projects that a consultant could encounter.
Example of reproducible results
Reproducible results are a lot more possible in conditions where the factors affecting the outcome can be controlled. For example, if one wanted to examine the effect of light, a fertilizer, and water on the growth of tomatoes, as long as one chooses plots of land that were similar in terms of light and irrigation and controlled the concentration of the fertilizer, then the results are much more likely to be reproducible. However, interventions that involve multiple confounding factors that are hard to control, are less likely to produce similar results.
However, one can always make an effort to minimize the effect of confounders. Examples include:
3. Does not knowingly conduct statistical practices that exploit vulnerable populations or create or perpetuate unfair outcomes.
The tragic tale of the Tuskegee Syphilis Study serves as an illustration1. From 1932 to 1972, the U.S. Public Health Service conducted a study on the progression of untreated syphilis in African American men in Macon County, Alabama. The participants were not informed of their diagnosis, not given informed consent, and were not provided with effective treatment even after penicillin was recognized as the standard of care for syphilis. The statistical data gathered from this unethical experiment was used in medical literature for many years, highlighting a significant abuse of trust and misuse of research ethics.
4. Opposes efforts to predetermine or influence the results of statistical practices and resists pressure to selectively interpret data.
Lack of understanding between practical and statistical significance is frequently a source of pressure from the client with respect to interpretation of the findings.
Example. No matter what statistical method is used, if the sample size is large enough, the standard error will decrease resulting in high t or F values and p-values less than 0.05, indicating statistical significance. Whereas, with large enough sample sizes, p-values less than 0.05 are highly likely to happen with low effect size, example lack of practical difference between the mean of the experimental and control group along with low power, or negligible R-squared in regression analysis, or very low accuracy in machine learning models such as logistic or ordinal regression.
Therefore, in situations with very large sample sizes leading to statistical but no practical significance, the statistical consultant needs to explain to the client that p-value smaller than 0.05 are necessary but not sufficient for reporting major findings and potentially asking for further funding.
5. Accepts full responsibility for their own work, does not take credit for the work of others, and gives credit to those who contribute. Respects and acknowledges the intellectual property of others.
Statistical consultation is not a solo process and always involves collaboration between a team of people. This was illustrated in the chapters that explain the multiple dimensions involved in successful team work including active listening, communication, having an internal locus of control, conflict resolution, empathy and team building. Thus, when a statistical consultant is asked to take the major role in designing a study, advise on development of instruments, data collection, analysis, and reporting of the data, the consultant should have respect for the work of all members and endorse them as needed.
6. Strives to follow, and encourages all collaborators to follow, an established protocol for authorship. Advocates for recognition commensurate with each person’s contribution to the work. Recognizes that inclusion as an author does imply, while acknowledgement may imply, endorsement of the work.
When it comes to publishing the work in refereed journal articles, the statistician along with the consultant need to:
What has to be pointed out is that the level of teaching and leadership that the statistical consultant is responsible for varies with the project. If the consultant is collaborating with researchers who are well known in the field, have an established theory, and want to find out the extent to which the data collected by them supports the hypotheses they want to prove, the role of the statistical consultant may be limited to advising the client on the right statistical method, overseeing analysis of the data, ascertaining that the relevant assumptions are met, and explaining the results in a language that is clear and understandable to the consumers of that research.
However, in some other projects, the statistical consultant is in the scene from the inception to the completion of the project. He may be asked to answer a question about a topic with which he is totally unfamiliar. He needs to spend some time learning about the topic, and then deciding what measurements he needs to add the relevant data. Then, the role of the statistical consultant would be different and a lot more guidance and leadership is expected.
Example. In a study (Esfandiari), the objective was to build a survey to measure ethics of elementary school children who were being taught how to create a business toward entrepreneurship. The first step was to invite a few major entrepreneurs and talk to them about the definition of “who is an entrepreneur” and what consists of ethical behavior in the field of entrepreneurship. After one-on-one interviews with these individuals and reading the relevant theory, it was decided that surveys would be useless for assessing the attitude of elementary school children toward entrepreneurship. Consequently, it was decided to create a survey that consisted of small case studies to which elementary school children could identify. For example, one item sounded as follows.
Mary sits at the same table with two more students, Jane and Deborah. Mary likes red pens but he does not have any. Jane has five red pens. Would be OK if Mary takes one of Jane’s red pens.
What was interesting was that after studying the literature, we found that one always needs to include one ethical, one unethical, and one conditionally ethical items because children who are at a certain age may not yet have developed the relevant schemata, menta structure, to have a full understanding and interpretation of abstract concepts such as ethical behavior.
7. Discloses conflicts of interest, financial and otherwise, and manages or resolves them according to established policies, regulations, and laws.
One of the major dimensions of EQ that was discussed in the prior chapter is learning about the constructive methods of “conflict resolution”, which is a field of itself in the world of today. We have experienced this multiple times in working with teams of students on the consultation process. We offer an example:
Example. In one of the projects during the pandemic the Asian students were interested in analyzing the president Trump’s tweets calling covid-19, the “Chinese Virus” and the ill effects that it had on the Asian community. Half of the team members were complaining that others had stolen the project and were running with it. The other half were complaining that they are not following anything and nobody answers their questions and they felt excluded.
To solve this issue, we took the following steps:
It looked like half of the students on this team were more advanced and were using methods like NLP (natural language processing), and similar techniques to analyze the tweets. Whereas, the other group members were not familiar with such techniques.
After talking to everyone, they decided to divide the task according to their qualifications and interest and it all worked well. However, the major thing was to use the right leadership, hear everyone, and guide them to come up with a decision that worked for the entire team.
8. Promotes the dignity and fair treatment of all people. Neither engages in nor condones discrimination based on personal characteristics. Respects personal boundaries in interactions and avoids harassment, including sexual harassment, bullying, and other abuses of power or authority.
In the time of post-Covid, one of the projects that the students worked on was a content analysis of Trump’s comments calling it the “Chinese Virus”. One group of Chinese enrolled students decided to take Trump’s tweets on this subject and employ different methodologies such as NLP (Natural Language Processing) and other related methods (Wordcloud, Sentiment Analysis) for finding patterns and development of predictive models of the relevant data. The group consisted of eight members and with two or three weeks to complete the project, half of the members complained that the other half had co-opted the project and they were not being treated as an integral part of the team.
In order to resolve this problem we first spoke to the members individually and tell their story. This needed 15-30 minutes per person, then we brought the entire group together to speak and listen to each other. The conclusion we reached was not one of disrespect or exclusion but instead some were more advanced in their techniques and were too excited about modeling. The others were not well trained enough to follow along and felt excluded.
We came to the conclusion that it would be best to divide the team into two separate groups and have each group work on a project tailored to their own interest. Both groups wound up being quite successful with equally sound results having used different datasets.
The conclusion reached was that in order to promote dignity and inclusion, it is best to invest time in listening to individuals and then to the entire group before making a decision as to what to do.
9. Take appropriate action when aware of deviations from these guidelines by others.
In the consulting course, we require students to give us the following:
All results must be reproducible, therefore all project work must have a clear workflow from raw data to final product. In this way, deviations can be identified quickly therefore it is not in any team’s interest to deviate from agreed guidelines and rules. In many projects, our clients continue to modify the raw data and desire to re-run the new data using the code provided by the teams.
10. Acquires and maintains competence through upgrading of skills as needed to maintain a high standard of practice.
In the consulting course, we ascertain that the students are able to integrate the previously acquired knowledge to solve real problems and also introduce additional more advanced methods to continue building their skills for specific clients.
Example. ROC are widely used in the biological sciences to report the accuracy of medical treatments as well as concepts such as false positive, true negative etc. Once students have demonstrated mastery they are better able to help our clients.
We are frequently the recipients of data from survey organizations within the university and outside. For example, student attitudes on current topics or educational interventions on changes in attitude. In our department, we do not offer courses in scale construction, generalizability theory, evaluation of reliability or factor or cluster analysis. We teach these methods in the consulting course encouraging continuing education and upgrading of skills.
11. Follows applicable policies, regulations, and laws relating to their professional work, unless there is a compelling ethical justification to do otherwise.
We train the students on the ethical practice of statistics and data science through having them read and analyze the relevant literature, view videos that demonstrate ethical practices in different disciplines, AI, medicine, law and business.
We further ascertain that the students have mastered these issues in ethical practice through team projects which teach/discuss ethical practice and the violation of them. Student teams then create videos which are used to demonstrate their mastery.
We have sought the permission of teams who created videos on “Ethical practice of data science in different disciplines” to use their videos as teaching tools. The link to some of these videos are given below:
For access to a collection of student team created videos please see: Ethics Playlist
12. Upholds, respects, and promotes these guidelines. Those who teach, train, or mentor in statistical practice have a special obligation to promote behavior that is consistent with these guidelines.
We promote and teach the ethical guidelines of ASA though the following channels
Example 1) Guidelines given to students and their teams for study:
Ethical Guideline II.A.7: The fact that a procedure is automated does not ensure its correctness or appropriateness; it is also necessary to understand the theory, data, and methods used in each statistical study. This goal is served best when a competent statistical practitioner is included early in the research design, preferably in the planning stage.*
Ethical Guideline II.A.8: Recognize that any frequentist statistical test has a random chance of indicating significance when it is not really present. Running multiple tests on the same data set at the same stage of an analysis increases the chance of obtaining at least one invalid result. Selecting the one “significant” result from a multiplicity of parallel tests poses a grave risk of an incorrect conclusion. Failure to disclose the full extent of tests and their results in such a case would be highly misleading.*
Ethical Guideline II.C.8:Clearly and fully report the steps taken to guard validity. Address the suitability of the analytic methods and their inherent assumptions relative to the circumstances of the specific study. Identify the computer routines used to implement the analytic methods.
Ethical Guideline II.C.12:Report the limits of statistical inference of the study and possible sources of error. For example, disclose any significant failure to follow through fully on an agreed sampling or analytic plan and explain any resulting adverse consequences.
Provide an example
Ethical Guideline II.D.2:Avoid the use of excessive or inadequate numbers of research subjects by making informed recommendations for study size. These recommendations may be based on prospective power analysis, the planned precision of the study endpoint(s), or other methods to ensure appropriate scope to either frequentist or Bayesian approaches. Study scope also should take into consideration the feasibility of obtaining research subjects and the value of the data elements to be collected.
Ethical Guideline II.G.3:Recognize that differences of opinion and honest error do not constitute misconduct; they warrant discussion, but not accusation. Questionable scientific practices may or may not constitute misconduct, depending on their nature and the definition of misconduct used.
Example 2) Examples of ethical dilemmas that have occurred as a result of breaching these guidelines
Case one. A client had one of the GSRs (graduate student researchers) in the consulting center analyze some data for him over a year ago. Then, he sent out the paper for publication and the reviewer suggested looking at the data in a different way. He wrote to me saying : “ If you remember you did this analysis and you were paid for it”. I have sent this paper for publication and the reviewer has asked a few questions about the analysis, can you write the answers?
I wrote back saying I did not do the analysis and in order to answer the questions asked by the editor and conduct the statistical analysis suggested by him, we need to make sure that certain assumptions are met. I contacted the former GSR and she said she could do this with a minimal fee (her old rate). He said: “I have no money and my institution cannot pay for it”. He wrote me about twenty emails and put me under pressure to write one sentence explaining to the editor why the method used was sufficient and that the method he is suggesting is not feasible. He did not stop until I told him what you are asking me is creating an ethical dilemma. He wrote back at least five times asking for one sentence as a response to the editor. I sent him some material to read about further assumptions that needed to be tested and he continued to ask for the one sentence that would convince the editor. I said what you are asking me to do is unethical. Simply tell the editor, in order to implement the method, you are suggesting certain assumptions need to be met.
This situation has been going on for over a month with multiple long emails over the break.
Identify the problem and analyze the situation.
Case two. A researcher wants to examine the effect of math anxiety on the performance of fifth graders on math exams. The fifth grade students in a local elementary school are randomly divided into two groups. The first group is told that the test is going to be very hard and they need to concentrate in order to do well. The second group is told that the test is not difficult and they should all be able to do well.
Case three. A workshop has been designed to increase the average sale of software by the employees that have been with the company for less than a year. Of the two hundred sellers that have worked for the company for a year, one hundred volunteered to participate in the workshop. The company reports the following results and claims that the workshop has been successful in increasing average sales.
Case four. A statistician is hired by a large chain to predict the average sale of electronics as a function of gender, years of experience, and level of interest in the job. He uses multiple regression to make the relevant model. He is given data on 2000 employees. He reports gender and years of experience not to be significant. He reports interest in the job to be significant. He reports R-squared to be 0.5 percent. He recommends to have the employees take a survey about their motivation for selling electronics prior to being hired.
Case five. A statistician who also teaches at a business school is hired to predict the annual salary in a major entrepreneurial foundation from years of education, years with the company, gender, ethnicity, and prior experience on the job. He uses the data in teaching his entrepreneurship course in the business school. The company writes a complaint to the dean of the business school.
Case six. A statistical consultant is given a large data set of 5000 patients with diabetic type II. He is asked to predict the factors that are related to diabetic type II. The consulting physician tells him the more factors he examines, the more informative the findings will be. He first uses logistic regression and finds hypertension, and smoking to be significant predictors and ethnicity not to be a significant predictor. However, when he uses chi-square he finds all three predictors to be significant. He conducts chi-square three times to examine the relationship between diabetic type II with smoking, hypertension, and ethnic background. He recommends using chi-square analysis.
Example 3) For homework we have them write reflection papers on ethical guidelines that have been jeopardized. An example: The following assignment was given to students after the lecture and discussion in data science. They are asked to watch YouTube one, which is an example of breaching the ethics of data science and discuss which ethical guidelines of ASA were broken. In the second part they are asked to watch a video on how scientists can breach the guidelines of ASA by creating fake data.

Example. Given the following case study, identify the potential sources of bias for data collection, summarization, processing, analysis, interpretation, and model development.
A team of six students enrolled in psychology 101 are required to carry out a project that involves multiple regression. They construct a survey on a Google form to predict academic success from fear of failure, gender, and major. They ask the students enrolled in the class to complete an anonymous survey. The respondents consist of 140 are social science, and 20 physical science majors. Of the 40 males 10 major in social sciences. Of the 140 social science majors, 120 are female and 20 are male.
After conducting multiple linear regression, they draw the following conclusions.
Fear of failure is the major cause behind academic success (significant correlation of - 0.2)
The effect of gender on academic success is higher for science majors. (significant results for gender and major and their interaction) See following table of means on academic success by major and gender.
R2 is 0.042 indicating that fear of failure, gender, major, and the combined effect of gender and major explain 4.2% of the variance in academic success. Given that all p-values are less than 0.05, we can conclude that the study has practical significance.

Example of unethical data summarization
Reporting mean as a measure of central tendency for small data set with outliers
Examples of unethical data processing
Combining data from studies that
Examples of unethical analysis
Repeating two-sample tests instead of carrying out one-way ANOVA
Repeating chi-square analysis instead of running a method like logistic regression
Using covariates as major predictors and claiming high practical significant
Examples of unethical interpretations
Inferring causality from correlational studies
Inferring causality from interventions that lack an acceptable control grou
Trying to interpret statistical findings
Examples of unethical model development
Using parametric statistical methods when the relevant assumptions are not met
Using statistical methods when the relevant assumptions are not met
Using statistical methods without sufficient knowledge of the mathematical underpinnings
